ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry specific to tapioca fritter; mapped to generic fritter class as closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tapioca (cassava starch products) — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for tapioca-based fried snack; no dedicated tapioca fritter code exists.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for tapioca fritter; A0CKP (tapioca/cassava starch) used as the nearest defensible parent anchor with deep-frying preparation context noted.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for tapioca fritter; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of tapioca fritter is one piece weighing approximately 60 g, commonly consumed as a morning snack or tea-time treat in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Tapioca Fritter — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tapioca Fritter (CR ID F11090062). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; tapioca (boiled) GI = 94 used as analogous estimate for deep-fried tapioca fritter
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tapioca fritter in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 94 is taken from the published value for boiled tapioca (cassava starch) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which is the closest approved analogous food. Deep-frying is not expected to lower GI relative to boiled tapioca; if anything, the batter coating may marginally increase the glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 94 × (40.0 × 0.60) / 100 = 94 × 24.0 / 100 = 22.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Borderline sodium at 220 mg/100 g combined with high saturated fat from deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit tapioca fritter to one small piece (≤60 g) per occasion, avoid salt-based dipping sauces, and account for its 132 mg sodium contribution per serving within a daily sodium budget of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (GI ≈ 94) from tapioca starch causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tapioca fritter is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high GI of approximately 94 and a glycemic load of 22.6 per 60 g serving; if consumed, restrict to a single small piece paired with a protein or fat source to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 4.8 g/100 g from deep-frying oil and batter, combined with high caloric density of 258 kcal/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit tapioca fritter consumption due to its saturated fat content (~4.8 g/100 g) and high caloric density; occasional small portions are acceptable within a diet otherwise low in saturated fat and refined carbohydrates.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tapioca fritter is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis because deep-frying generates AGEs that exacerbate joint inflammation; baked or steamed tapioca preparations are preferred alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tapioca Fritter (Cucur Ubi Kayu) is a popular Singaporean and Southeast Asian deep-fried snack made from tapioca (cassava) coated in a wheat flour batter and fried until golden and crispy. The exterior is crunchy and lightly battered, while the interior is soft, chewy, and mildly sweet from the natural sugars in tapioca. It is commonly sold at hawker centres, pasar malam (night markets), and roadside stalls as a breakfast or tea-time snack. Per 100 g, it provides 258 kcal, 40 g carbohydrate, 10.1 g fat (approximately 4.8 g saturated), 1.9 g protein, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 220 mg sodium. The glycemic index is very high (approximately 94), making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or blood glucose levels. The deep-frying process increases caloric density and generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs), which are of concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease. Sodium is borderline at 220 mg/100 g, warranting portion control for those with hypertension. Despite its high-GI profile, the 3.7 g dietary fibre per 100 g provides modest digestive benefit. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-friendly but contains gluten from the wheat flour batter.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a tapioca fritter?
Is tapioca fritter high in carbs?
Is tapioca fritter suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of tapioca fritter?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F11090062) with confidence 0.95. GI estimated from the closest approved analogous food (boiled tapioca, GI 94) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed publication. Saturated fat estimated at 4.8 g/100 g based on typical deep-fried tapioca preparation using palm or vegetable oil; this is consistent with the total fat of 10.1 g/100 g. Multilingual names verified for Singaporean cuisine category.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific tapioca fritter preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F11090062) or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 2. saturated_fat_g of 4.8 g/100 g is estimated from typical deep-fried tapioca preparation using palm/vegetable oil, consistent with total fat 10.1 g/100 g; this value is not explicitly reported in the HPB source and should be confirmed when detailed fatty acid data become available. 3. potassium_mg of 200 mg/100 g is an estimate based on raw cassava/tapioca composition from USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, cassava raw ~271 mg/100 g, reduced by cooking and frying losses); used to support hypertension offset assessment. 4. GI of 94 is an analogous estimate from boiled tapioca (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for tapioca fritter exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Dietary tags 'wholegrain' and 'high_fibre' from the input record have been reviewed: 'wholegrain' is not applicable to tapioca fritter (tapioca is a refined starch, not a wholegrain) and has been removed. 'high_fibre' is borderline at 3.7 g/100 g (threshold typically ≥6 g/100 g for high-fibre claim) and has been removed from dietary flags. 6. FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as nearest defensible anchor; no dedicated tapioca fritter code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026-04-16.
